macro_segments.title: The Dispute over Stipulated Conditions: When a Person Restricts Their Vow to Only Half the Obligation

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Mishna's Rule that Todah Cannot Come from Ma'aser Sheni Unless Explicitly Stipulated
Gemara's Question: Why Force Someone to Bring the Full Todah When They Only Want the Todah Without Bread
Chizkiyah's Explanation: This Is Beit Shammai's Position That Words Bind Regardless of Intent
Rabbi Yochanan's Alternative: This Could Even Follow Beit Hillel If the Person Explicitly Stated They Would Bring the Full Todah If Required
The Baraita's Example of Someone Saying Todah Without Bread, Then Claiming Ignorance of the Full Requirement
Abuye and Rava on the Phrase Shamor Veshamata: Observe What You Said and Listen to What the Torah Requires

macro_segments.title: Stipulation to Use Ma'aser Sheni Money: The Permissibility and Proper Method of Using Tithed Funds for Obligatory Sacrifices

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: If Someone Says They Will Bring Todah From Ma'aser Sheni Money by Stipulation, This Is Permitted
Gemara's Question: Does Yavi Mean an Obligation to Use Ma'aser or Merely Permission?
Rav Papa and Rav Chisda: Yavi Means Permission to Use Ma'aser If You Want; It Creates an Option, Not an Obligation
The Restriction: Do Not Bring Todah Bread From the Actual Grain of Ma'aser Sheni; Only From Money Converted From Ma'aser Sheni
Rav Nachman and Rav Chisda: The Grain Itself of Ma'aser Sheni Cannot Be Used for Todah Bread, Only Grain Purchased With Ma'aser Sheni Money
Rabbi Zeira's Stricter View: Even Grain Purchased With Ma'aser Sheni Money Cannot Be Used; Only the Bread Itself

macro_segments.title: The Conceptual Dispute: Why Wheat and Flour Cannot Be Used Despite Being Purchased With Ma'aser Sheni Money

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmeyah Reports Rabbi Zeira's Reasoning: Todah Cannot Come From Grain Purchased With Ma'aser Sheni Money
Rabbi Zeira's First Rationale (Per Rabbi Yirmeyah): Todah Is Learned From Shelamim, and Shelamim Are Never Ma'aser Themselves
Rabbi Zeira's Counter-Rationale: Wheat Could Have Been Ma'aser, So We Prohibit Using Purchased Wheat to Avoid Doubling Up Ma'aser Identity
The Implications: If Bread Cannot Be Made From Grain or Flour Purchased With Ma'aser Sheni Money, How Is It Obtained?

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Doubling Up Sanctities: Money Cannot Simultaneously Hold Ma'aser Sheni and Shelamim Sanctity

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ammi: When You Sanctify Ma'aser Sheni Money With Shelamim Sanctity Directly, the Shelamim Sanctity Does Not Take Effect
The Reason: The Sanctity of Shelamim Is Not Strong Enough to Override the Sanctity of Ma'aser Sheni
The Distinction: Using Money to Purchase an Animal Avoids Identity Conflict, But Directly Sanctifying Money Creates a Contradiction
The Baraita on Improper Use: Buying a Wild Animal for Shelamim or a Domestic Animal for Regular Meat From Ma'aser Sheni Money
The Principle: When a Purchase Is Not the Normal Use of Ma'aser Sheni, the Skin Does Not Become Desanctified

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Whether Ma'aser Sheni Money Can Be Sanctified for Shelamim: Rav Nachman and Rav Chisda vs. Rabbi Zeira; Rabbi Yochanan vs. Rabbi Elazar

micro_segments.title: Another Debate: Can You Directly Sanctify Ma'aser Sheni Money for Shelamim, or Does the Sanctity Not Take Effect?
Framing the Dispute: Does Ma'aser Sheni Constitute Private Money or Temple Money? Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Meir
According to Rabbi Yehuda (Ma'aser Is Private Money), Even Rabbi Meir's Position Would Allow Sanctifying for Shelamim
According to Rabbi Meir (Ma'aser Is Temple Money), One Opinion Says You Cannot Sanctify It for Shelamim; Another Says You Can via Natural Purpose
The Baraita's Evidence: A Baraita Assumes You Can Sanctify Ma'aser Sheni Money for Shelamim and Must Add Two-Fifths Upon Redemption
The Gemara's Resolution: This Baraita Follows Rabbi Yehuda's Position; It Does Not Contradict Rabbi Meir's View

macro_segments.title: The Principle That Obligatory Sacrifices Cannot Come From Ma'aser Sheni: Applying the Pesach Paradigm to All Korbanot

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's General Principle: Davar She'bechova (Obligatory Sacrifice) Cannot Come From Ma'aser Sheni; Learned From Pesach
The Gezeirah Shava: Just as Pesach Is an Obligation and Comes Only From Chullin, So All Obligatory Korbanot Come Only From Non-Sanctified Funds
Nesachim: Libations Never Come From Ma'aser Sheni Money, Even Without an Obligation, Because No One Eats Them
How Do We Know Pesach Cannot Come From Ma'aser Sheni? A Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva Over Learning From Pesach Mitzrayim to Pesach L'Doros

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer: We Learn the Rule for All Future Pesach From Pesach Mitzrayim, Which Did Not Come From Ma'aser Sheni
Rabbi Akiva's Challenge: You Cannot Learn From an Impossible Case (No Ma'aser Sheni in Egypt) to a Possible Case
Rabbi Eliezer's Counter: Even an Impossible Case Can Serve as Strong Proof of the Principle
Rabbi Akiva's Second Attack: Pesach Mitzrayim Had No Blood on the Altar, So It Cannot Serve as Model for Later Pesach
Rabbi Eliezer's Final Response: The Phrase Ve'avadta Links All Future Pesach to the Original, Binding Their Laws Together
Rav Sheshet: Once a Pasuk Explicitly Links Two Cases, Logical Objections Cannot Override It—Ein Meshibin Al Ha'Heikesh

topical_tags.term: Mamon Hedyot vs. Mamon Gavoah (Private vs. Temple Money)
Ein Meshibin Al Ha'Heikesh (Logical Objections Cannot Override Explicit Pasuk Link)
